primary_diagnosis.name: Acute flare of Microscopic Colitis (Mild Collagenous)

pathophysiology_summary: Microscopic (collagenous) colitis is a chronic inflammatory condition of the colon defined histologically by a thickened sub-epithelial collagen band and a lymphocytic infiltrate, with a macroscopically normal mucosa. The collagen band and mucosal inflammation impair colonic water and electrolyte reabsorption, producing chronic or episodic secretory, non-bloody watery diarrhoea. Flares are usually self-limiting over 24-72 hours with symptomatic care, but a high-frequency acute flare causes real fluid and electrolyte loss — the principal danger in this presentation, especially in an older adult living alone, is hypovolaemic dehydration and its consequences (orthostatic hypotension, falls, acute kidney injury). The clinically important reasoning task is that the reassuring euvolaemic findings documented in person (no orthostatic drop, capillary refill 2s, moist mucous membranes, soft non-peritonitic abdomen) are exactly the data a telehealth encounter cannot obtain, so via chat the flare attribution remains presumptive and significant dehydration and the dangerous mimics (C. difficile colitis, ischaemic colitis, surgical abdomen) cannot be excluded remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden high-frequency watery diarrhoea after dinner is most commonly infective until the history narrows it.

differential.diagnosis: Hypovolaemic dehydration from acute diarrhoeal losses
differential.evidence_basis: Postural dizziness and profound weakness in an older adult after ~10 watery motions raises immediate concern for volume depletion.

differential.diagnosis: Flare of known chronic microscopic colitis
differential.evidence_basis: Patient reports a biopsy-diagnosed chronic watery-diarrhoea condition — plausible but must not be assumed before dangerous causes are screened.
Acute colitis in an older adult must prompt a C. difficile screen even without a clear antibiotic history.
Older adult with acute abdominal pain and diarrhoea — must be considered, especially if blood appears or pain is out of proportion.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, an infective/dehydration frame should lead; anchoring immediately on the known benign condition is premature closure.

differential.diagnosis: Flare of known chronic microscopic colitis
differential.evidence_basis: Non-bloody watery diarrhoea, no fever, no antibiotics or travel, and a matching biopsy-proven history make a flare of the known condition the best fit.

differential.diagnosis: Hypovolaemic dehydration from acute diarrhoeal losses
differential.evidence_basis: Regardless of the underlying cause, postural dizziness plus high stool frequency means hydration status is the safety-critical unknown.
Made less likely by the absence of fever and recent antibiotics, but not formally excluded without stool testing.
Argued against by non-bloody stool and pain that is not out of proportion, but cannot be excluded remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: History narrows toward a flare, but hydration remains the not-to-miss item and the infective/ischaemic mimics stay on the list until tested.

differential.diagnosis: Flare of known chronic microscopic colitis
differential.evidence_basis: No bleeding, no fever, no melaena, still passing urine — consistent with a benign flare of the known condition.

differential.diagnosis: Significant hypovolaemic dehydration / early hypovolaemic shock
differential.evidence_basis: Cannot be confirmed or excluded via telehealth — orthostatic vitals, capillary refill and mucous-membrane assessment are unobtainable over chat, so it must be treated as a live possibility.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen is reassuring for the mimics but does NOT resolve the hydration question — the crux of the case.

differential.diagnosis: Acute flare of microscopic (collagenous) colitis, presumptive, with dehydration not excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Best clinical fit, but confirmation of euvolaemia and exclusion of C. difficile/ischaemic colitis require in-person assessment and stool/blood tests that telehealth cannot provide.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth conclusion is a presumptive flare that still mandates same-day in-person hydration assessment; a confident 'benign flare, manage at home' conclusion over-reaches the available evidence.

red_flags.description: Postural dizziness and profound weakness ('can barely stand') in an older adult after high-frequency diarrhoea — possible orthostatic hypovolaemia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests meaningful volume depletion; the confirming/excluding signs (orthostatic BP, capillary refill, mucous membranes) cannot be obtained via chat, so dehydration cannot be ruled out remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration overnight in someone living alone, with orthostatic collapse, fall/injury or acute kidney injury and no one present to summon help.

red_flags.description: High-frequency watery diarrhoea (~10 motions in 5 hours) with ongoing losses
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sustained high-volume losses drive dehydration and electrolyte disturbance, particularly in an older adult.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-estimating total fluid loss and failing to arrange assessment or rehydration.

red_flags.description: Bright red blood or black tarry (melaena) stool
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presence would point to GI bleeding or ischaemic colitis and demand emergency assessment; its absence is reassuring but must be actively screened and re-checked (note bismuth can blacken stool and mask melaena).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing an evolving GI bleed or ischaemic colitis.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Presence would raise concern for invasive bacterial colitis, C. difficile or systemic sepsis; its absence lowers but does not eliminate that risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing evolving sepsis or C. difficile colitis in an older adult.

red_flags.description: Anuria / no urine passed for more than 8 hours (or markedly reduced output)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A key bedside proxy for significant dehydration and acute kidney injury; currently reassuring (still voiding) but a critical safety-net trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to established AKI from unrecognised hypovolaemia.

red_flags.description: Confusion, disorientation, or a fall from dizziness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate severe dehydration/hypoperfusion or injury and require an emergency ambulance response, especially in someone alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised severe dehydration or an unwitnessed fall with injury.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring / premature closure on the known diagnosis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting the patient's biopsy-proven chronic condition as 'just a flare, manage at home' and skipping the dehydration and dangerous-mimic screen.

Treating the case as if the in-person reassuring signs (no orthostatic drop, capillary refill 2s, moist mucous membranes, soft abdomen) are known — they are unobtainable over chat and must not be assumed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-estimating dehydration in the older adult living alone
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Discounting postural dizziness and high stool frequency because the patient is alert and conversing; older adults decompensate later and this one has no one at home overnight.
Failing to actively elicit the pertinent negatives (blood, fever, antibiotics, travel) that keep these not-to-miss mimics on or off the list.
Not warning that bismuth blackens the stool, which can hide the black tarry appearance that would otherwise flag GI bleeding.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Mucous-membrane moisture and skin turgor / hydration status on examination
Abdominal examination (tenderness, guarding, rebound, bowel sounds)
Stool microbiology — C. difficile toxin/PCR and culture
Serum electrolytes, urea and creatinine (renal function) and full blood count
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment to obtain orthostatic vitals and hydration status that chat cannot provide, and to consider stool studies and bloods; escalate immediately to 000/ED if any red flag appears (bright red blood or black tarry stool, high fever, no urine for >8 hours, confusion, or a fall/collapse). Support oral rehydration in the meantime.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High — the benign known diagnosis strongly invites anchoring, yet the very findings that made home management safe in person are unobtainable remotely, so the flare must remain presumptive and dehydration cannot be excluded via chat.
